 {"id":"bd-1mh","title":"Update session picker to use SQLite index","description":"# Update session picker to use SQLite index

## Goal
Replace filesystem walking in session picker with fast SQLite index queries.

## Current Implementation (src/session_picker.rs)
Currently scans filesystem:
```rust
// Slow: walks all session directories
let sessions = walkdir::WalkDir::new(&sessions_dir)
    .into_iter()
    .filter_map(|e| e.ok())
    .filter(|e| e.path().extension() == Some("jsonl"))
    .collect();
```

## New Implementation
```rust
use crate::session_index::SessionIndex;

impl SessionPicker {
    pub fn load_sessions(cwd: Option<&str>) -> Result<Vec<SessionMeta>> {
        let index = SessionIndex::open_default()?;
        index.list_sessions(cwd)
    }
}
```

## UI Changes
- Display indexed metadata directly (no file reads needed)
- Show message count from index
- Show session name from index
- Faster render (no filesystem I/O)

## Fallback Behavior
If index unavailable or empty:
```rust
fn load_sessions_with_fallback(cwd: Option<&str>) -> Result<Vec<SessionMeta>> {
    // Try index first
    if let Ok(index) = SessionIndex::open_default() {
        if let Ok(sessions) = index.list_sessions(cwd) {
            if !sessions.is_empty() {
                return Ok(sessions);
            }
        }
    }
    
    // Fallback to filesystem (and populate index)
    let sessions = load_sessions_from_filesystem(cwd)?;
    // Optionally trigger reindex in background
    Ok(sessions)
}
```

## Dependencies
- bd-3nz (indexing must work first)

## Testing
- Test: Session picker shows indexed sessions
- Test: Picker falls back to filesystem if needed
- Test: Performance improved (measure time)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Picker queries index by default
- [ ] Fallback to filesystem works
- [ ] UI displays index metadata
- [ ] Noticeably faster for 50+ sessions","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:46.861448590Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:59.101819926Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1mh","depends_on_id":"bd-346","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:46.870643664Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1mh","depends_on_id":"bd-3nz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:59.101795250Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2jh","title":"Implement error handling path tests","description":"# Implement error handling path tests

## Goal
Test all error conditions that can occur during normal operation, with detailed logging.

## Background
Error paths are currently untested but critical for UX:
- Network failures
- API rate limits
- Authentication failures
- Malformed responses
- File system errors
- Timeout scenarios

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- All API error cases must use VCR playback (no live network).
- File system errors must use temp dirs and deterministic paths.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log cassette path, error category, and sanitized error details.
- On failure, dump expected vs actual error variants + any response body fragments.

## Test Categories

### Network Errors
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_connection_refused() {
    let vcr = VcrRecorder::new("network_connection_refused");
    let provider = AnthropicProvider::new_with_client(vcr.client());

    let result = provider.stream(&context, &options).await;
    assert!(matches!(result, Err(Error::Network { .. })));
}
```

### API Errors
- 429 rate limit (retry-after)
- 401 auth failure
- 403 forbidden
- 400 bad request
- 500 server error
- 529 overloaded

### Malformed Response Errors
- Invalid JSON body
- Missing required fields
- Unexpected SSE event type
- Truncated stream

### File System Errors
- Read permission denied
- Write permission denied
- Disk full (simulate with tempfs limits if feasible)
- Path too long

### Tool Execution Errors
- bash command not found
- bash timeout
- edit file not found

## Dependencies
- bd-1pf (VCR infrastructure for API error testing)

## Files
- tests/error_handling.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 25+ error handling tests
- [ ] All network errors covered (via VCR/local simulation)
- [ ] All API error codes covered
- [ ] File system errors covered
- [ ] Tool errors covered
- [ ] Logs include error category + cassette id
- [ ] Error messages are helpful
- [ ] All tests pass","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:40:33.110276433Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T05:43:34.660912407Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2jh","depends_on_id":"bd-1pf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:41:01.345001879Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2jh","depends_on_id":"bd-26s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:40:33.123723281Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2jh","depends_on_id":"bd-30u","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T05:43:34.660884836Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2jh","depends_on_id":"bd-3ml","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T05:43:32.812857475Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2lg","title":"Implement compaction module unit tests","description":"# Implement compaction module unit tests

## Goal
Add comprehensive unit tests for src/compaction.rs (903 lines, currently 0 tests).

## Background
Compaction is a critical feature that:
- Summarizes old conversation history to save context tokens
- Identifies optimal cut points in message history
- Tracks file operations (read/write/edit) for summary
- Uses LLM to generate summaries

## Module Analysis (src/compaction.rs)

### Key Types
```rust
struct CompactionContext {
    entries: Vec<SessionEntry>,
    config: CompactionConfig,
    token_estimator: TokenEstimator,
}

struct CutPoint {
    entry_index: usize,
    reason: CutPointReason,
    tokens_before: usize,
    tokens_after: usize,
}

enum CutPointReason {
    TokenLimit,
    UserMessageBoundary,
    ToolResultBoundary,
    NaturalBreak,
}

struct FileSummary {
    path: String,
    operations: Vec<FileOp>,
}
```

### Key Functions
- `estimate_tokens(entry: &SessionEntry) -> usize`
- `find_cut_points(entries: &[SessionEntry], config: &Config) -> Vec<CutPoint>`
- `extract_file_operations(entries: &[SessionEntry]) -> Vec<FileSummary>`
- `prepare_compaction(entries: &[SessionEntry], config: &Config) -> CompactionPlan`
- `generate_summary_prompt(entries: &[SessionEntry], files: &[FileSummary]) -> String`

## Test Categories

### 1. Token Estimation Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_estimate_tokens_simple_text() {
    let entry = user_message("Hello, world!");
    let tokens = estimate_tokens(&entry);
    assert!(tokens > 0 && tokens < 10);
}

#[test]
fn test_estimate_tokens_code_block() {
    let entry = assistant_message("```rust\nfn main() {}\n```");
    let tokens = estimate_tokens(&entry);
    // Code blocks should estimate higher
}

#[test]
fn test_estimate_tokens_tool_result() {
    let entry = tool_result("read", "Large file content...");
    let tokens = estimate_tokens(&entry);
    // Verify reasonable estimate
}
```

### 2. Cut Point Detection Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_find_cut_points_token_limit() {
    let entries = generate_entries(100, 1000);  // 100 entries, ~1000 tokens each
    let config = CompactionConfig { max_tokens: 50000 };
    
    let cuts = find_cut_points(&entries, &config);
    
    // Should find cuts when exceeding limit
    assert!(!cuts.is_empty());
    assert!(cuts[0].tokens_after <= config.max_tokens);
}

#[test]
fn test_cut_point_prefers_user_message_boundary() {
    let entries = vec![
        user_message("Q1"),
        assistant_message("A1"),
        user_message("Q2"),  // Preferred cut point
        assistant_message("A2"),
    ];
    
    let cuts = find_cut_points(&entries, &low_limit_config());
    
    // Should cut at user message boundary
    assert_eq!(cuts[0].reason, CutPointReason::UserMessageBoundary);
}

#[test]
fn test_cut_point_preserves_tool_pairs() {
    let entries = vec![
        user_message("Read file"),
        assistant_with_tool_call("read", { "path": "test.txt" }),
        tool_result("read", "File contents..."),
        assistant_message("The file contains..."),
    ];
    
    let cuts = find_cut_points(&entries, &config);
    
    // Should not cut between tool call and result
    for cut in cuts {
        assert_ne!(cut.entry_index, 2);  // Not after tool call
    }
}
```

### 3. File Operation Extraction Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_extract_file_operations_read() {
    let entries = vec![
        tool_result_with_details("read", json!({"path": "src/main.rs", "lines_read": 100})),
    ];
    
    let files = extract_file_operations(&entries);
    
    assert_eq!(files.len(), 1);
    assert_eq!(files[0].path, "src/main.rs");
    assert_eq!(files[0].operations[0], FileOp::Read { lines: 100 });
}

#[test]
fn test_extract_file_operations_write() {
    let entries = vec![
        tool_result_with_details("write", json!({"path": "new.txt", "bytes_written": 1234})),
    ];
```

## Goal
Define theme JSON schema and implement theme file loading.

## Implementation

### Theme Types (src/theme.rs)
```rust
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
use std::path::{Path, PathBuf};

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Deserialize, Serialize)]
pub struct Theme {
    pub name: String,

    pub syntax: SyntaxColors,
    pub ui: UiColors,
}

pub struct ThemeColors {
    pub foreground: String,
    pub background: String,
    pub accent: String,

    pub warning: String,
    pub error: String,
    pub muted: String,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Deserialize, Serialize)]
pub struct SyntaxColors {
    pub keyword: String,
    pub string: String,
    pub number: String,
    pub comment: String,
    pub function: String,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Deserialize, Serialize)]
pub struct UiColors {
    pub border: String,
    pub selection: String,
    pub cursor: String,
}
```

### Theme Discovery
```rust
impl Theme {
    pub fn discover_themes() -> Vec<PathBuf> {
        let mut paths = Vec::new();
        
        // Global themes
        if let Some(home) = dirs::home_dir() {
            let global = home.join(".pi/agent/themes");
            if global.exists() {
                paths.extend(glob_json(&global));
            }
        }
        
        // Project themes
        let project = PathBuf::from(".pi/themes");
        if project.exists() {
            paths.extend(glob_json(&project));
        }
        
        paths
    }
    
    pub fn load(path: &Path) -> Result<Self, Error> {
        let content = fs::read_to_string(path)?;
        let theme: Theme = serde_json::from_str(&content)?;
        theme.validate()?;
        Ok(theme)
    }
    
    pub fn load_by_name(name: &str) -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Search discovered themes for matching name
        for path in Self::discover_themes() {
            if let Ok(theme) = Self::load(&path) {
                if theme.name == name {
                    return Ok(theme);
                }
            }
        }
        Err(Error::ThemeNotFound(name.to_string()))
    }
}
```

### Validation
```rust
impl Theme {
    fn validate(&self) -> Result<(), Error> {
        // Validate all color strings are valid hex
        self.validate_color(&self.colors.foreground)?;
        self.validate_color(&self.colors.background)?;
        // ... etc
        Ok(())
    }
    
    fn validate_color(s: &str) -> Result<(), Error> {
        if !s.starts_with('#') || s.len() != 7 {
            return Err(Error::InvalidColor(s.to_string()));
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Goal
Replace reqwest usage in src/providers/anthropic.rs with asupersync HTTP client.

## Background
Anthropic is the most complex provider implementation:
- Extended thinking support (streaming thinking deltas)
- Cache control headers (ephemeral caching)
- Tool use with JSON schema
- Multiple content block types in responses
- 748 lines of code, 2 unit tests currently

Current reqwest usage (lines to change):
- Line ~200: reqwest::Client creation
- Line ~250: client.post(url).json(&request)
- Line ~280: response.bytes_stream() for SSE
- Various header handling

## Implementation Steps

1. **Replace Client Creation**
```rust
// Before (reqwest)
let client = reqwest::Client::new();

// After (asupersync)
use crate::http::Client;
let client = Client::new()?;
```

2. **Replace Request Building**
```rust
// Before
let response = client
    .post(&url)
    .header("x-api-key", &api_key)
    .header("anthropic-version", "2023-06-01")
    .json(&request)
    .send()
    .await?;

// After
let response = client
    .post(&url)
    .header("x-api-key", &api_key)
    .header("anthropic-version", "2023-06-01")
    .json(&request)
    .send(&cx)
    .await?;
```

3. **Replace SSE Streaming**
```rust
// Before (reqwest-eventsource or manual)
let mut stream = response.bytes_stream();
while let Some(chunk) = stream.next().await { ... }

// After (asupersync SSE adapter)
use crate::http::SseStream;
let mut sse = SseStream::new(response);
while let Some(event) = sse.next(&cx).await {
    match event? { ... }
}
```

4. **Update Function Signatures**
```rust
// Add Cx parameter to stream()
async fn stream(
    &self,
    cx: &Cx,  // New parameter
    context: &Context,
    options: &StreamOptions,
```

-{"id":"bd-37z","title":"Spec: Hostcall ABI + capability manifest","description":"# Goal
Define a **precise hostcall ABI** and **capability manifest** so every extension I/O is explicit, auditable, and enforceable.

# Why
- Our replacement for Node/Bun hinges on `host.call(...)` and explicit capabilities.
- This is the security and compatibility backbone; ambiguity here will leak later.

# Scope / Deliverables
- Request/response schema: `id`, `capability`, `method`, `params`, `timeout_ms`, `cancel_token`.
- Error taxonomy: deterministic mapping for timeouts, denied capability, IO failure.
- Streaming contract: chunked responses and backpressure signals (if used).
- Capability manifest schema: declarative list of required connectors + scope (paths, hosts, methods).
- Evidence ledger format: per-hostcall log entry with capability, args hash, and timing.

# Security Invariants

 {"id":"bd-9sa","title":"Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper","description":"# Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper

## Goal
Create a functional HTTP client in src/http/client.rs using asupersync APIs.

## Current State
src/http/client.rs has:
- Client struct (empty)
- RequestBuilder struct with method/url/headers/body
- RequestBuilder::send() returns Err("asupersync HTTP client not yet implemented")
- RequestBuilder::json() silently ignores serialization errors

## Implementation Requirements

### Client API
```rust
use asupersync::{Cx, http::client::HttpClient};
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnectorBuilder;

pub struct Client {
    inner: HttpClient,
}

impl Client {
    pub fn new() -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Create TLS connector with native roots
        let tls = TlsConnectorBuilder::new()
            .with_native_roots()?
            .build()?;
        let inner = HttpClient::new(tls);
        Ok(Self { inner })
    }
    
    pub fn request(&self, method: Method, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder {
        RequestBuilder::new(self, method, url)
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
    pub fn post(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
}
```

### RequestBuilder API
```rust
impl RequestBuilder {
    pub fn header(mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn json<T: Serialize>(mut self, body: &T) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn body(mut self, bytes: Vec<u8>) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn timeout(mut self, duration: Duration) -> Self { ... }
    
    pub async fn send(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<Response, Error> {
        // Actually send the request using asupersync
    }
}
```

### Response API
```rust
pub struct Response {
    status: StatusCode,
    headers: Headers,
    body: BodyReader,  // Async streaming body
}

impl Response {
    pub fn status(&self) -> StatusCode { ... }
    pub fn headers(&self) -> &Headers { ... }
    pub async fn text(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<String, Error> { ... }
    pub async fn json<T: DeserializeOwned>(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<T, Error> { ... }
    pub fn bytes_stream(self) -> impl Stream<Item = Result<Bytes, Error>> { ... }
}
```

## Key Considerations
1. **TLS**: Use rustls with native root certificates (no openssl)
2. **Timeout**: Support per-request timeout via asupersync Budget
3. **Streaming**: Response body must support async streaming for SSE
4. **Headers**: Support standard headers (Content-Type, Authorization, etc.)
5. **Error handling**: Wrap asupersync errors in pi's Error type

## Testing
- Unit test: Client creation succeeds
- Unit test: RequestBuilder builds correct request
- Integration test: GET request to httpbin.org/get
- Integration test: POST with JSON body
- Integration test: Timeout behavior

 {"id":"bd-ah1","title":"Implement session persistence cycle tests","description":"# Implement session persistence cycle tests

## Goal
Test the full session lifecycle: create → save → reload → verify integrity, with detailed logs.

## Background
Session persistence involves:
- JSONL file format (one JSON object per line)
- Header with metadata (version, cwd, timestamp)
- Message entries with parent/child relationships
- Tree structure for conversation branching
- File locking for concurrent access

Current tests cover serialization but not full I/O cycles.

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- Use temp dirs and deterministic paths; avoid asserting exact timestamps.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log session path, header fields, message IDs, tree shape, and file sizes.
- On failure, dump the JSONL contents (redacted) and parsed header.

## Test Categories

### 1. Basic Persistence Cycle Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_session_save_and_reload() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project/path");
    session.add_user_message("Hello");
    session.add_assistant_message("Hi there!");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.messages().len(), 2);
}
```

### 2. Tree Structure Persistence Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_branching_persists() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project");
    let root_id = session.add_user_message("Root");
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch A");

    session.navigate_to(root_id.clone());
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch B");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    let children = loaded.children_of(&root_id);
    assert_eq!(children.len(), 2);
}
```

### 3. Header Metadata Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_header_metadata_preserved() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/my/project/path");
    session.set_provider("anthropic");
    session.set_model("claude-opus-4");
    session.set_thinking_level(ThinkingLevel::High);
    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.cwd(), "/my/project/path");
}
```

### 4. Entry Type Persistence Tests
- ModelChange entries
- ThinkingLevel entries
- Compaction entries
- Tool invocation entries (if serialized)

### 5. Error Handling Tests
- Missing file
- Corrupted JSONL
- Partial corruption (valid header + bad line)

### 6. Concurrent Access Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_concurrent_writes_locked() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let session1 = Session::new("/project");
    session1.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle1 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 1");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle2 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 2");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let (r1, r2) = join(handle1, handle2);
    assert!(r1.unwrap().is_ok() || r2.unwrap().is_ok());
}
```

### 7. Directory Structure Tests
- Persist creates nested directories when needed

 {"id":"bd-btq","title":"Extensions: Node/Bun-free runtime (PiJS) — prove superior path","description":"# Goal
Deliver a Node/Bun-free extension runtime (PiJS) that is **more secure, more deterministic, and faster** than legacy Node/Bun extensions **without losing compatibility** for representative real-world extensions.

# Background / Motivation
- Mario’s critique is correct: QuickJS lacks Node/Bun OS wrappers and event loop. We replace that with a **minimal, capability-gated PiJS runtime + deterministic event loop**.
- We want a system that feels *better* to extension authors: clearer errors, explicit permissions, and reproducible behavior.

# Strategy (PiJS)
1) **Connector-first architecture** — all I/O is explicit `host.call` into the connector dispatcher; no ambient OS APIs.
2) **Minimal JS stdlib** — `pi.fs`, `pi.http`, `pi.timers`, `pi.path`, `pi.env`, `pi.crypto`, `pi.time` mapped to connectors.
3) **Deterministic event loop** — microtasks, timers, hostcall promises driven by asupersync with strict ordering rules.
4) **Compatibility layer** — extc rewrite + shims; unsupported APIs rejected with actionable diagnostics.
5) **Security + performance proof** — capability gating, resource limits, benchmarks with reproducible outputs.
6) **Developer UX** — structured logs + trace viewer + remediation hints.
7) **Testing** — comprehensive unit, conformance, and E2E scripts with detailed logging artifacts.

# Success Criteria
- Representative JS extensions run without Node/Bun.
- Deterministic ordering across repeated runs (trace equality).
- Capability policy enforced for every hostcall with structured logs.
- Benchmarks meet or beat budgets.
- Evidence binder (docs + logs + benchmarks) is reproducible.

# Non-Goals
- Full Node/Bun parity.
- Implicit global OS access.

# Notes for Future Self
This is the umbrella for the **"prove them wrong"** path.
